explanation: For each value of i (1, 2, 3) the inner loop runs with j = 1 then j = 2, printing i*j each time: i=1 gives '1 2 ', i=2 gives '2 4 ', i=3 gives '3 6 ', for a final output of '1 2 2 4 3 6 '. Option B reverses which variable is the outer; C lists sequential integers 1–6 ignoring the product; D shows only the j=2 products.

The outer loop runs 4 times (i = 0, 1, 2, 3) and the inner loop completes all 3 of its iterations for each outer iteration, so count++ executes 4 × 3 = 12 times. Option A adds the two bounds instead of multiplying; C squares the outer bound (4×4); D is an off-by-one of the correct answer.

The outer loop runs 5 times and the inner loop runs 5 times for each outer iteration, so the body executes 5 × 5 = 25 times total. Option A only counts the outer iterations; B adds the two bounds (5+5); C would result from a 4×5 loop (an off-by-one error on one dimension).

Because the inner loop condition is `j <= i`, it runs i times on each outer iteration: i=1 prints '1 ', i=2 prints '1 2 ', i=3 prints '1 2 3 '. Each System.out.println() ends the line. Option B confuses printing j with printing i; C uses j <= 3 (constant) instead of j <= i; D would require the outer condition to be `i < 3`.

The inner loop adds 1, then 1+2, then 1+2+3 across the three outer iterations: i=1 adds 1 (sum=1); i=2 adds 1+2 (sum=4); i=3 adds 1+2+3 (sum=10). Option A computes only 1+2+3=6, ignoring the repeated lower terms; B and D are plausible arithmetic errors that don't match the actual triangular accumulation.

The condition `c <= 3` causes the inner loop to execute for c = 0, 1, 2, 3, printing four values per row instead of three. Changing to `c < 3` gives exactly three values (0, 1, 2) per row. Option B produces 4 rows instead of 3; C starts at c=1, printing '1 2 3 ' per row; D places a newline after every single value.

The condition `i == j` is true only at the four diagonal pairs (0,0), (1,1), (2,2), (3,3), each printing i once, giving '0 1 2 3 '. Option B would result from printing on every iteration of both loops; C would result from stopping after the first match; D shifts each value up by 1 as if the loops started at 1.

Because the inner loop starts at j = i, the inner iteration counts decrease: i=1 runs 4 times, i=2 runs 3, i=3 runs 2, i=4 runs 1, totaling 4+3+2+1 = 10. Option D treats both loops as running n=4 times each (4×4=16); A computes 4+4 instead of n(n+1)/2; C is a common off-estimate for triangular sums.

Only even j values trigger x++. i=1: j={1}, 0 even → x=0. i=2: j={1,2}, j=2 is even → x=1. i=3: j={1,2,3}, j=2 is even → x=2. i=4: j={1,2,3,4}, j=2 and j=4 are even → x=4. Option A stops counting after i=3; B undercounts the i=4 row; D overcounts by treating all rows as contributing two even values.

For each i (1, 2, 3) the inner loop prints i+j for j = 1, 2, 3: i=1 gives '2 3 4 ', i=2 gives '3 4 5 ', i=3 gives '4 5 6 '. Option B shows multiplication (i*j); C would be correct if j re-initialized to i+1 each time; D shifts each group up by 1 as if i started at 2.

stem: Which of the following best describes the execution order of a nested loop?

options.label: A
options.text: The outer loop completes all of its iterations before the inner loop begins executing.

options.label: B
options.text: The inner loop completes all of its iterations for each single iteration of the outer loop.

options.label: C
options.text: The total number of inner loop body executions equals the sum of the outer and inner loop counts.

options.label: D
options.text: The inner loop and outer loop always execute the same number of times.

explanation: In a nested loop, each time the outer loop body executes the entire inner loop runs to completion before the outer loop advances to its next iteration. Option A reverses the relationship; C mistakes multiplication for addition (total executions is the product when loops are independent); D is only true if both loops happen to share the same bounds.

options.label: A
options.text: Change the outer loop condition from `i <= n` to `i < n`

options.label: B
options.text: Change the inner loop initialization from `j = 1` to `j = i`

options.label: C
options.text: Change the inner loop condition from `j <= n` to `j <= i`

options.label: D
options.text: Move `System.out.println()` to before the inner loop

explanation: As written the inner loop always runs n=3 times, producing a 3×3 square. Changing `j <= n` to `j <= i` makes the inner loop run exactly i times per outer iteration, giving 1, 2, then 3 asterisks across the three rows. Option A reduces to 2 rows; B starts j at i but still ends at j <= n, producing a decreasing (not increasing) triangle; D places a blank line before each row of stars.

The outer loop runs 5 times (i = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4) and for each outer iteration the inner loop also runs 5 times (j = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4), giving 5 × 5 = 25 total calls. A counts only the outer loop iterations. B adds the two loop bounds (5 + 5) instead of multiplying them. C reflects an off-by-one error where the student counts one of the loops as running only 4 times.

When i=1, the inner loop runs 5 times (j=1..5); when i=2 it runs 4 times (j=2..5); continuing, the counts are 5+4+3+2+1 = 15, a triangular number equal to n(n+1)/2 = 5×6/2. A (10) results from starting j at i+1 instead of i, giving 4+3+2+1+0 = 10. C (20) results from an off-by-one on the inner upper bound, e.g., using `j <= 6`. D (25) assumes both loops always run 5 times (the rectangular case).

With i and j starting at 0, the expression i*3+j+1 produces exactly the values 1–9 in row-major order: (i=0,j=0)→1, (i=0,j=2)→3, (i=1,j=0)→4, (i=2,j=2)→9. B uses i+j+1, which prints 1,2,3 for the first row but then 2,3,4 for the second row. C uses i*j+1, giving 1,1,1 for the entire first row because i=0 makes every product 0. D computes (i+1)*(j+1), the multiplication table, yielding 1,2,3 then 2,4,6 then 3,6,9.

Because `temp` is declared inside the outer loop, it resets to 0 at the start of every outer iteration. Each time, the inner loop computes temp = 1+2+3 = 6, so every line prints the current `i` paired with 6. B results from declaring `temp` before the outer loop so it accumulates across iterations (6, 12, 18). C results from misreading the inner loop as `j <= i`, running fewer iterations each row and building a partial triangle sum. D results from confusing `+=` with `=`, storing only the final value of j (which is 3) rather than the running sum.

For each outer value of i (0, 1, 2), j takes values 0, 1, 2 and prints i+j: i=0 yields 0 1 2; i=1 yields 1 2 3; i=2 yields 2 3 4, giving the full sequence. A prints i three times per outer iteration (ignores j's contribution to the sum). C treats the nested structure as a single loop printing only distinct totals. D prints j alone each time (ignores i).

The double sum of i*j factors as (1+2+3)×(1+2+3) = 6×6 = 36. A (18) results from adding only i on each inner iteration (sum += i), so 3×(1+2+3) = 18. B (30) is an off-by-one error where j starts at 2 instead of 1, skipping the j=1 terms. D (45) equals 1+2+…+9, as if the products were replaced by a flat sequence from 1 to 9.

The outer loop runs 6 times and the independent inner loop runs 4 times per outer iteration, giving 6 × 4 = 24 total executions. A (10) adds the loop bounds instead of multiplying. B (30) is 6 × 5, as if the inner bound were j <= 5 (one too many). C (36) uses the outer bound for both loops (6 × 6).

The condition i == j is satisfied exactly when both indices are equal: (0,0), (1,1), (2,2), (3,3) — four times. B (0) results from believing the condition is never true. C (12) equals 4×4 − 4, the count of pairs where i ≠ j, as if the condition were negated. D (16) counts every iteration of the inner loop regardless of the condition.

The inner loop runs i times for each outer value of i, and it always prints i: i=1 contributes one "1"; i=2 contributes two "2"s; i=3 three "3"s; i=4 four "4"s. A assumes the inner loop runs exactly once per outer iteration. B is an off-by-one error — using j < i instead of j <= i causes i=1 to produce zero prints, shifting everything. D prints j instead of i, producing a triangular sequence of j values.

When i=1, j runs from 2 to 5 (4 lines); i=2 gives 3 lines; i=3 gives 2; i=4 gives 1; i=5 gives 0. Total: 4+3+2+1 = 10 = n(n−1)/2. A (15) equals n(n+1)/2, the result if j started at i rather than i+1. C (20) doubles the correct answer, as if each ordered pair were printed twice. D (25) is n², ignoring the dependence of the inner bound on i.
The inner loop runs 1, 2, and 3 times for `r` values 1, 2, and 3, adding 1, then 2 and 3, then 3, 4, and 5 for a total of 18. The other values come from common off-by-one traces of the inner loop.

options.label: A
B
C

explanation: For each row `r`, the inner loop must print the numbers from 1 through `r`. Choice B stops too early, choice C starts at 0, and choice D prints four numbers on every row.
